11: hymin to mysterion dedotai tes basileias tou theou — to you the mystery of the kingdom of God has been given — mysterion (mystery) in Jewish apocalyptic usage is the hidden divine plan that is disclosed only to the elect (Daniel 2:18, 27-30; 1QpHab 7). The disclosure is a gift (dedotai, divine passive) not an achievement. ekeinois de tois exo en parabolais ta panta ginetai — but for those outside everything is in parables — the binary (inside/outside) reflects the community's self-understanding as those who have received the revelation that the world has not; the parables function simultaneously as revelation (for insiders) and concealment (for outsiders who refuse).
26: hos anthropos bale ton sporon epi tes ges kai katheude kai egeire nykta kai hemeran kai ho sporos blastan kai mekynetai — the kingdom of God is as if a man scatters seed on the ground and sleeps and rises night and day and the seed sprouts and grows he knows not how — the unique feature of this Markan-only parable is the farmer's passivity: the growth occurs automatē (Mark 4:28, of itself, automatically, the origin of our word automatic). The kingdom's growth is not a human program but a divine process; human beings plant and wait but do not control the growth.
1: Kai palin Erxato didaskein para ten thalassan — and again he began to teach beside the sea — palin (again) connects to the lakeshore teaching of Mark 2:13; the outdoor setting by the lake is established as a regular venue. Kai synagetai pros auton ochlos pleistos, hOste auton eis ploion emBanta kathEsthai en te thalasse — and the largest crowd yet gathered to him, so that he got into a boat and sat in the sea — the superlative pleistos (very large, greatest crowd) signals escalation. The boat-as-podium removes Jesus from the pressing crowd (cf. Mark 3:9) while allowing him to address everyone on the shore. The spatial arrangement — speaker in the boat, audience on the beach — creates an amphitheater effect, the water carrying his voice.
2: Kai edidAsken autous en parabolais polla — and he was teaching them many things in parables — the imperfect edidasken (was teaching, ongoing) with polla (many things) establishes the parabolic teaching as a sustained mode, not a single occasion. Parabolē (parable — from para + ballō, to throw alongside) is the placing of two things side by side for comparison: the earthly scene (farming, fishing, domestic life) placed alongside the kingdom reality to illumine it. Kai elegen autois en te didachE autou — and he was saying to them in his teaching — the editorial transition signals that a representative parable follows.
3: AkouEte — Listen! — the imperative opening is the parable's own call for the kind of attention that will yield understanding. The same verb akouO (to hear) appears at Mark 4:9 (ho echōn Ōta akouein akouetō = whoever has ears to hear let him hear) and Mark 4:12 (the Isaiah hearing-without-understanding quotation). The entire parable section is framed by the call to hear and the paradox of hearing without understanding. Idou exElthen ho speirOn speirai — behold a sower went out to sow — idou (behold) invites visual imagination. The historic present exElthen makes the scene immediate. The sower's identity is left entirely ambiguous until the interpretation in Mark 4:14 (the sower sows the word).
4: Kai egeneto en tO speirein ho men epesen para ten hodon — and as he sowed, some fell beside the road — the fourfold soil sequence begins. Para ten hodon (beside/along the road) — the packed earth of a footpath through or beside the field: impenetrable to the seed, which lies on the surface. Kai Elthen ta peteina kai katephagen auto — and the birds came and ate it up — the immediate consumption leaves no trace of the seed's landing. The interpretation (Mark 4:15) identifies the birds with Satan who removes the word before it can take root.
5: Kai allo epesen epi to petrOdes — and other seed fell on the rocky ground — petrOdes (rocky, full of rock — from petra, rock/bedrock) is soil with a thin layer over bedrock, not soil full of scattered stones. Hopou ouk eichen gen pollen, kai euthys exanEtEilen dia to mE echein bathos gEs — where it did not have much soil, and immediately it sprang up because it had no depth of soil — the speed of germination (euthys) is caused by the shallowness: without the need to penetrate deeply, the seed springs up quickly. The quick appearance is deceptive — the depth required for sustaining the plant is absent.
6: Kai hote anEteilen ho Elios ekauma tIsthE kai dia to mE echein rizan exEranthE — and when the sun rose it was scorched, and because it had no root it dried up — ekauma tIsthE (was scorched — kaumatizO from kauma, burning heat) and exEranthE (was dried up — the same root xErainO as the withered hand of Mark 3:1). The absence of root (rizan) is the lethal condition: the apparent vitality of Mark 4:5 (springing up quickly) masks the structural deficiency. The interpretation (Mark 4:17) identifies this with those who respond with joy but have no root and fall away under persecution.
7: Kai allo epesen eis tas akanthas kai anebEsan hai akanthai kai synEpnixan auto kai karpon ouk edOken — and other seed fell into the thorns and the thorns came up and choked it and it did not give grain — synEpnixan (choked, strangled — from sympnigO, to choke together) describes the thorns growing up simultaneously with the grain and crowding it out. Karpon ouk edOken (gave no fruit/grain) is the diagnostic outcome: growth occurred but the productive end was prevented. The interpretation (Mark 4:19) identifies the thorns with the worries of the age, the deceitfulness of wealth, and desires for other things.
8: Kai alla epesen eis ten gen ten kalEn kai ediDou karpon anabainonta kai auxanomena kai epheren en triakonta kai en hexEkonta kai en hekaton — and other seed fell into the good soil and was giving fruit, coming up and growing, and was bearing thirty and sixty and a hundredfold — the good soil produces triple gradations of yield: 30, 60, 100. The imperfect ediDou (was giving, kept giving) indicates sustained fruitfulness rather than a single harvest. The yields are remarkable by ancient agricultural standards (typical yields were 5-10 fold); the 30/60/100 is not uniformly extraordinary but varies — even the lowest good-soil yield is notable. The interpretation (Mark 4:20) simply names the outcome: they hear, accept, and produce a crop.
9: Kai elegen, Ho echOn Ōta akouein akouetO — and he was saying: "whoever has ears to hear let him hear" — the hearing-call concludes the parable and picks up the opening imperative of Mark 4:3 (akouete). The idiom ho echOn Ōta (having ears) distinguishes the capacity for hearing from the mere anatomical possession of ears: the point is that spiritual perception is a gift or condition, not automatic. The reflexive structure (akouein akouetO — hearing/let-him-hear) intensifies: the one who can hear should really hear. The same formula appears in 4:23 and throughout the Gospels and Revelation (Mark 2:7,11,17,29 etc.).
10: Kai hote egeneto kata monas, ErOtOn auton hoi peri auton syn tois dOdeka tas parabolas — and when he was alone, those around him along with the Twelve were asking him about the parables — kata monas (alone, apart — the idiom for private withdrawal from the public) creates the inner-circle setting for the explanation. The questioners are broader than just the Twelve: hoi peri auton (those around him) is the wider circle of committed followers, with the Twelve as the core. They ask about tas parabolas (the parables, plural) — not just the sower parable but the entire parabolic teaching mode. The question occasions the explanation of the parabolic method (Mark 4:11-12) before the specific sower-interpretation (Mark 4:13-20).
12: Hina blepOntes blepOsin kai mE idOsin, kai akOuontes akOuOsin kai mE syniOsin, mEpote epistrepsOsin kai aphethe autois — in order that seeing they may see and not perceive, and hearing they may hear and not understand, lest they should turn and be forgiven — the hina (in order that) introduces a purpose clause citing Isaiah 6:9-10 — one of the most theologically difficult verses in the Gospels. The hardening purpose of parables: the parables are not merely pedagogy for the slow but vehicles of concealment for those who have placed themselves outside. Three proposed readings: (1) the hina is genuinely purposive (God's sovereign hardening, cf. Rom 9-11); (2) the hina translates an Aramaic de- that can be either purpose or result; (3) the context qualifies the hardening as God's response to existing rejection. The Isaianic echo is intentional: Mark's Jesus stands in the line of Isaiah, whose proclamation both revealed and concealed.
13: Kai legei autois, Ouk oidate tEn parabolEn tautEn, kai pOs pasas tas parabolas gnOsesthe? — and he said to them: "do you not know this parable? then how will you know all the parables?" — OuK oidate (do you not know/understand) implies the disciples' failure to grasp the parable is a failure that calls for explanation. The meta-question — "if you don't get this parable, how will you understand any parable?" — establishes the sower parable as foundational: it is the key parable, the one whose interpretive logic (hearing the word, response types, fruit or no fruit) governs all the others. Understanding this parable is prerequisite to understanding the parabolic method as a whole.
14: Ho speirOn ton logon speirei — the sower sows the word — the interpretation's first move is the identification of the seed: ton logon (the word, the gospel message). The sower's identity remains ambiguous — Jesus, the disciples, any preacher. The interpretive focus falls entirely on the soil (the receiver-types), not on the sower. Ton logon is the technical term for the early Christian message (Acts 4:4; Mark 6:2,4; Mark 8:4; 1 Thessalonians 1:6), indicating that the parable was read in the early church as a map of mission-experience: why does the same proclamation produce such different results?
15: Houtoi de eisin hoi para ten hodon hopou speiretai ho logos, kai hotan akOusOsin, euthys erchetai ho Satanas kai airei ton logon ton esparmenon eis autous — these are the ones along the road where the word is sown; and when they hear, immediately Satan comes and takes away the word that was sown in them — euthys erchetai ho Satanas (immediately Satan comes) — the euthys of demonic activity mirrors the euthys of Jesus's own activity. The immediacy of removal before the word can take root is the mechanism of the first soil's failure: the word never gets the chance to germinate. The Satanic taking-away is a forcible removal (airei = takes up, removes), not a gradual fading.
16: Kai houtoi eisin homoiOs hoi epi ta petrOdE speiromenoi, hoi hotan akOusOsin ton logon euthys meta charas lambanousin auton — and these likewise are the ones sown on the rocky places, who when they hear the word immediately receive it with joy — the joy-reception (meta charas lambanousin auton) is the initial positive response that makes the rocky-ground hearer the most deceptive soil-type. The outward response is genuinely positive: immediate, joyful reception. Euthys again — the same immediacy that marks both Satanic removal (Mark 4:15) and the quick germination of shallow soil (Mark 4:5). Speed without depth is the danger.
17: Kai ouk echousin rizan en heautois alla prosKairoi eisin, eita genomenes thlipseOs E diOgmou dia ton logon euthys skandalizontai — and they do not have root in themselves but are temporary; then when affliction or persecution comes because of the word, immediately they are caused to stumble — prosKairoi (temporary, time-limited — pros + kairos: for a season) contrasts with the aiOnios (eternal, age-long) character of what genuine discipleship produces. SkandalizontaI (are scandalized, caused to stumble — from skandalon, a trap-trigger or stumbling stone) is the technical term for apostasy in the face of pressure. The dia ton logon (because of the word) specifies that the persecution is a direct response to the gospel commitment.
18: Kai alloi eisin hoi eis tas akanthas speiromenoi — and others are those sown among the thorns — the third soil-type receives its own interpretive verse (Mark 4:18) and an expanded list of the thorn-competitors in Mark 4:19. The naming of three specific "thorns" (worries, wealth-deceit, desires) is the most detailed interpretation among the four soils, suggesting that this type was particularly recognizable and dangerous in the early church context.
19: Hai merimnaI tou aiOnos kai hE apatE tou ploutou kai hai peri ta loipa epithymiai eisporeuomenai sympnigousin ton logon kai akarpos ginetai — the worries of the age and the deceitfulness of wealth and the desires for other things, entering in, choke the word and it becomes unfruitful — three named thorns: merimnaI tou aiOnos (anxieties of the age), apatE tou ploutou (deceitfulness/seduction of wealth — apatE is active deception, not mere distraction), and hai peri ta loipa epithymiai (desires concerning other things). The common thread is displacement: the word is not opposed by these forces but crowded out. The final diagnosis (akarpos ginetai = becomes unfruitful) echoes the plant that gives no grain in Mark 4:7.
20: Kai ekeinoi eisin hoi epi ten gen ten kalEn sparentes, hoi akOuousin ton logon kai paradechontai kai karpophorousin en triakonta kai en hexEkonta kai en hekaton — and those are the ones sown on the good soil, who hear the word and accept it and bear fruit, thirty and sixty and a hundredfold — the fourfold sequence climaxes in the good soil. ParadechontaI (accept, receive alongside — para + dechomai, to receive and hold onto) distinguishes genuine reception from the surface-acceptance of Mark 4:16 (lambanousin). The triple yield (30/60/100) mirrors Mark 4:8: genuine fruitfulness varies in degree but is genuinely fruitful at every level. The good-soil hearer is not an idealized perfect disciple but one who hears, receives, and actually produces.
21: Kai elegen autois, MEti erchetai ho lychnos hina hypo ton modion tethE E hypo ten klinEn, ouch hina epi ten lychian tethE? — and he said to them: "the lamp is not brought in to be put under the bushel-basket or under the bed, is it? It is to be put on the lampstand, isn't it?" — lychnos (oil lamp) placed on the lychnia (lampstand, the raised holder for maximum illumination) — the household logic is self-evident. Modion (bushel, grain-measure container) and klinE (bed/couch) are the two unlikely lamp-hiding places: the lamp would be smothered under the basket and dangerously misused under the bed. The lamp has an inherent telos — to give light — and hiding it defeats its purpose.
22: Ou gar estin ti krypton ean mE hina phanerOthE, oude egeneto apokryphon all' hina elthE eis phaneron — for nothing is hidden except to be made manifest, and nothing has been concealed except to come into the open — the purpose clause (hina phanerOthE ... hina elthE eis phaneron) is key: hiddenness is not an end state but a temporary condition aimed at future disclosure. This reframes the parable-concealment of Mark 4:11-12: the mystery is hidden for a season (the pre-resurrection period), not permanently suppressed. The lamp saying and this disclosure saying together form a corrective to any misreading of Messianic-Secret concealment as permanent.
23: Ei tis echei Ōta akouein, akouetO — if anyone has ears to hear, let him hear — the call to hearing appears for the second time in the chapter (cf. Mark 4:9), creating a bracket around the explanation of the parabolic method (Mark 4:10-22). The repetition reinforces the central concern of the chapter: hearing is the critical faculty and the greatest variable in the reception of the word. The parable of the soils, the lamp, and the seed-growth are all variations on the theme of reception-and-response.
24: Kai elegen autois, BlepEte ti akouEte. En O metrO metrEite metrEthEsetai hymin kai prostEthEsetai hymin — and he was saying to them: "pay attention to what you hear; with the measure you use it will be measured to you, and more will be added to you" — blepEte ti akouEte (pay attention to what you hear — blepO used for the quality of hearing, not merely its occurrence) combines the two primary senses for careful engagement. The measure-for-measure principle (en O metrO metrEite metrEthEsetai hymin) is a well-known Jewish proverbial saying (Mishnah Sotah Mark 1:7; Matthew 7:2 applies it to judgment); here it is applied to receptivity: the more attentive and active your hearing, the more you will receive.
25: Hos gar echei, dothEsetai autO kai hos ouk echei, kai ho echei arthEsetai ap' autou — for whoever has, it will be given to him; and whoever does not have, even what he has will be taken from him — the paradoxical give/take saying appears in three Synoptic contexts (Matthew 13:12; Mark 25:29; Luke 8:18; Mark 19:26). In the context of hearing-receptivity: those who engage the word with active hearing receive more; those who receive it passively (or not at all) lose even the superficial reception they had. The saying applies the economic logic of interest-bearing investment: capital that is used grows; capital that is hoarded or unused is lost. Spiritual receptivity operates on the same principle.
27: Kai katheude kai egeirEtai nykta kai hEmeraEn kai ho sporos blastA kai mEkynEtai hos ouk oiden autos — and he sleeps and rises night and day and the seed sprouts and grows, he himself does not know how — hos ouk oiden autos (he himself does not know how) — the farmer's ignorance of the growth mechanism is the central point. The growth is not his achievement or his understanding; it occurs independently of his knowledge. This is the parable's unique Markan contribution: not just the patience of waiting (Matt/Luke do not include this parable) but the specific emphasis on the farmer's non-comprehension. Kingdom growth operates beyond human understanding as well as human control.
28: AutomatE hE gE karpophorei, prOton chorton, eita stachyn, eita plEre siton en tO stachyi — the earth produces fruit by itself: first the stalk, then the head, then the full grain in the head — automatE (by itself, automatically — from autos + matos/madness, hence "self-acting") is a NT hapax in this sense (2 Peter 2:14 and Acts 12:10 use it for automatic gates). The word emphasizes the intrinsic generative power of the earth-seed relationship that operates without human intervention. The three stages (chortos = grass/blade, stachys = head of grain, plErEs sitos = full mature grain) describe the organic sequence of grain development: the growth is orderly, progressive, and internally driven.
29: Hotan de paradO ho karpos, euthys apostellei to drepanon, hoti parEstEken ho therismos — but when the fruit allows/permits, immediately he sends the sickle because the harvest has come — paradO ho karpos (when the fruit yields/delivers itself — paradidOmi used of the grain: it "gives itself over") is a vivid personification of the ripened grain's readiness. ApostellEi to drepanon (sends the sickle) echoes Joel 3:13 (aposteilate drepana hoti parEstEken therismos = send the sickle because the harvest has arrived), which is the eschatological harvest of judgment. The harvest-language frames the kingdom's growth as moving toward the final divine reckoning that brings the hidden growth into the open.
30: Kai elegen, POshomoiOsOmen tEn basileian tou theou E en tini autEn parabolE thOmen — and he was saying: "how shall we liken the kingdom of God or with what parable shall we put it?" — the double question (pOs homoiOsOmen ... en tini ... thOmen?) is a rabbinic formula for introducing a parable: the question invites the hearer into the process of analogy-selection, making the comparison active rather than passive. The first-person plural (homoiOsOmen ... thOmen = how shall we liken, what shall we use?) includes the hearer in the act of analogizing. The self-reflective character of the question marks the mustard seed parable as consciously parabolic.
31: Hos kokkO sinapEOs, hos hotan sparE epi tes gEs, mikroteron on pantOn tOn spermatOn tOn epi tes gEs — like a mustard seed, which when sown upon the earth, is the smallest of all the seeds upon the earth — the mustard seed's proverbial smallness is the starting point. MikroteroN ... pantOn tOn spermatOn (smallest of all the seeds) is not a botanical claim about absolute minimum seed-size but a proverbial expression for extreme smallness (the mustard seed was the standard smallest-unit in rabbinic measurement: "the tiniest of tiny things"). The point is the contrast between starting-size and ending-size, not a claim to botanical precision.
32: Kai hotan sparE, anabainei kai ginetai meizon pantOn tOn lachanOn kai poiei kladous megalous, hOste dynasthai hypo ten skian autou ta peteina tou ouranou kataskenoun — and when sown it comes up and becomes the greatest of all the garden plants and produces large branches, so that the birds of the sky can nest in its shade — meizon pantOn tOn lachanOn (greatest of all the garden plants) — garden mustard (sinapi) can reach 2-3 meters; not a tree but large enough for the comparison. The birds nesting in the branches echoes Daniel 4:12 LXX (Nebuchadnezzar's tree-dream) and Ezekiel 17:23; Mark 31:6 — the great empires as world-trees sheltering all nations. The kingdom-as-mustard-seed that becomes a bird-sheltering plant is a deliberately modest counter-image to the imperial tree-dream: the kingdom is not violent conquest but organic growth.
33: Kai toiautais parabolais pollais elalei autois ton logon, kathOs Edynanto akouein — and with many such parables he was speaking the word to them, as they were able to hear — kathOs Edynanto akouein (as they were able to hear) is the Markan editorial key to the parabolic method: the content and form of teaching is calibrated to the audience's current receptive capacity. This is not condescension but accommodation — meeting hearers where they are. The parables are not barriers to understanding but steps toward it, provided the hearer exercises the ears to hear (Mark 4:9,23).
34: ChOris de parabolEs ouk elalei autois, kat' idian de tois idiois mathEtais epelyen panta — but without a parable he was not speaking to them; but privately to his own disciples he was explaining everything — the public/private distinction: for the crowd, always parables; for the inner disciples, private explanation (kat' idian). Epelyen panta (was explaining everything — epilyO = to solve, unloose, explain, the same root as lyo = to loose) completes the hermeneutical privilege of the insider: the disciples receive not just the parable but its key. This is not arbitrary exclusion but the logic of covenantal disclosure to those who have committed themselves to the teacher.
35: Kai legei autois en ekeinE tE hEmera opsias genomenes, Dielthōmen eis to peran — and he said to them on that day, when evening came: "let us cross over to the other side" — en ekeinE tE hEmera (on that day) connects the crossing to the lakeside parabolic teaching of the day; the journey begins at the day's end (opsias genomenes). Dielthōmen eis to peran (let us go through to the other side) — the peran (the other side) is the Decapolis region, Gentile territory east of the lake. The geographical movement from Jewish Galilee toward Gentile territory mirrors the parabolic movement from concealment toward disclosure.
36: Kai aphentes ton ochlon paralambAnousin auton hOs En en tO ploiO — and leaving the crowd, they took him along just as he was in the boat — hOs En (just as he was) is the vivid Markan detail: Jesus comes aboard the boat without preparation, in whatever condition the day's teaching left him. Kai alla ploiari En met' autou — and other boats were with him — this detail is unique to Mark and never explained; the other boats simply appear and disappear. Their presence gives the scene the texture of an actual recollection rather than an idealized account.
37: Kai ginetai lailaps megalE anemou, kai ta kymata epeballen eis to ploion, hOste Ede gemizesthai to ploion — and a great windstorm arose, and the waves were breaking over the boat, so that the boat was already being swamped — lailaps (squall, violent windstorm — used in 2 Peter 2:17 for tempests; a more vivid word than seismos/storm) is specifically a sudden violent gust. The Sea of Galilee is known for sudden storms caused by temperature differentials between the water and surrounding hills. Ede gemizesthai (already being filled, being swamped) — the danger is immediate and concrete: the boat was already taking on water.
38: Kai autos En en tE prymne epi to proskephalaion katheudOn — and he himself was in the stern on the cushion sleeping — prymna (the stern, the back of the boat) is the helmsman's position; the proskephalaion (cushion, headrest — probably the leather rowing cushion or helmsman's seat cushion) is a vivid detail of physical exhaustion. The sleeping Jesus in the storm is both the image of utter physical depletion (the day's teaching has exhausted him) and the Christological paradox: the one who commands the sea (Mark 4:39) sleeps through its rage. Kai egeirousin auton kai legousin autO, Didaskale, ou melei soi hoti apollymetha? — and they woke him and said: "Teacher, don't you care that we are perishing?" — melei soi (does it concern you? do you care?) is urgent appeal, not rebuke.
39: Kai diegertheis epetimEsen tO anemO kai eipen tE thalassE, SiOpa, pephimOso — and having been aroused he rebuked the wind and said to the sea: "Be quiet! Be muzzled!" — epetimEsen (rebuked — the exorcism verb, epitimaO, used in 1:25 for rebuking the unclean spirit) applied to the wind: Jesus addresses the storm with the same authority-command used in exorcisms. SiOpa (be quiet, be silent) + pephimOso (be muzzled — perfect passive imperative of phimoO, the same muzzle-verb of 1:25 used on the demon) — the identical double command: Jesus silences the storm the same way he silenced the demon. Kai ekopasen ho anemos kai egeneto galEnE megalE — and the wind ceased and there was a great calm — the galEnE megalE (great calm) is the Christological sign: the sea obeys as the demonic forces obey.
40: Kai eipen autois, Ti deiloi este? OupO echete pistin? — and he said to them: "why are you cowardly? Do you not yet have faith?" — deiloi (cowardly, timid — from deilos, used in Revelation 21:8 for the category of the faithless who are excluded from the new city) is a strong word: not merely "afraid" (phobos) but specifically cowardly under trial. OupO echete pistin (not yet have faith) — the oupO (not yet) is important: it is not a permanent verdict but a diagnosis of the disciples' current stage. The storm-fear is not irrational (the boat was sinking) but is diagnosed as a failure of faith-confidence in the one who is with them.
41: Kai ephobEthEsan phobon megan kai elegon pros allElous, Tis ara houtos estin hoti kai ho anemos kai hE thalassa hypakouei autO? — and they were seized with great fear and were saying to one another: "who then is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?" — ephobEthEsan phobon megan (feared with great fear — cognate accusative for emphasis) — the fear after the calming is greater than the fear during the storm: awe before the divine presence exceeds terror before natural danger. Tis ara houtos estin (who then is this?) — the Christological question the disciples cannot yet answer. It is the question Mark poses to the reader throughout the Gospel. Kai ho anemos kai hE thalassa hypakouei autO — even the wind and the sea obey him — the hypakouO (to hear under, obey) used for the demons in 1:27 is now used for the elements of creation.
